metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete boolean detection expression for Anthropic's refusal-as-content case (e.g., `finish_reason == 'content_filter' AND len(text_parts) > 0`, OR `finish_reason is normal AND provider_details.get('<key>') == '<value>'`), matching the pydantic-ai 1.107.0 observed field names from the experiment log.
findings.finding: The Anthropic refusal-as-content detection condition is underspecified: the plan says `finish_reason` normalizes to `content_filter` for Anthropic AND `provider_details` 'may carry' the raw stop_reason, but never states the concrete boolean expression an executor must implement for the Anthropic path — specifically whether the trigger is `finish_reason == 'content_filter' AND non_empty_text_parts`, or whether `provider_details` must also be inspected when `finish_reason` is normal.
findings.location: What section — Refusal-as-content protocol paragraph: 'for Anthropic (rebar's provider), getattr(result.response,\'finish_reason\',None) normalizes a refusal to content_filter and provider_details (a dict, present on ModelResponse) may carry the raw stop_reason'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line pseudocode expression to the spec, e.g.: 'Anthropic refusal-as-content fires when `finish_reason in ("content_filter",) AND any text part is non-empty; if Anthropic returns a normal finish_reason with a refusal in content, the provider_details key to inspect is `<key>`.' This disambiguates from the existing check_stop_reason path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) strike 'wall-clock timeout' from the plan title and scope it explicitly as a hoopoe dependency in AC5 ('RED silent-success cases flip GREEN — silent-hang case contingent on story hoopoe'), or (b) include a minimal per-request timeout in this story's scope.
findings.finding: The plan title and 'Why' section list wall-clock timeout as one of this story's three silent-success detection mechanisms, but the body delegates it entirely to story hoopoe ('The per-request/tool timeout from story hoopoe is the silent-hang backstop'). AC5 ('RED silent-success cases flip GREEN') does not carve out the hang case, so the AC would be unverifiable for silent hangs if hoopoe has not yet landed — a cross-section contradiction between the stated scope and the verifiable acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Plan title / 'Why' section vs body ('per-request/tool timeout from story hoopoe') vs AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan title change 'wall-clock timeout + verdict schema-shape + structured refusal-as-content' to 'verdict schema-shape + structured refusal-as-content (wall-clock timeout via story hoopoe)'; add a parenthetical to AC5: 'RED silent-success cases for refusal-as-content and truncation flip GREEN; silent-hang coverage is contingent on story hoopoe landing first.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the `provider_details` field path for Anthropic refusal-as-content detection in a verifiable repo artifact (e.g., a test fixture that captures a real or canned Anthropic refusal ModelResponse and asserts the field structure), not solely in the plan's experiment-log prose.
findings.finding: The plan's Anthropic refusal-as-content detection rule asserts that `ModelResponse.provider_details` (described as 'a dict, present on ModelResponse') 'may carry the raw stop_reason' for out-of-band Anthropic filter signals. No source file in the repo reads `provider_details` from any ModelResponse, and no test exercises this field. The entire premise rests solely on the plan's own 'Verified library behavior' prose block — no repo artifact (fixture, lockfile, test data, import trace) grounds it.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol' paragraph; 'Verified library behavior' block
findings.suggested_fix: Add a canned-response test (alongside the existing canned `content_filter`/`length` tests in `test_llm_failure_matrix.py`) that constructs a mock `ModelResponse` with non-empty text parts and a `provider_details` dict in the expected shape, and asserts the refusal-as-content detector fires. This both proves the field exists in the expected form and pins the contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the refusal-as-content detection predicate precisely: state that the check fires only when `finish_reason` is NOT already in `_UNRETRYABLE_STOP_REASONS` (i.e. a 'normal' stop) AND `provider_details` carries the out-of-band filter signal, and give the exact key path (e.g. `provider_details.get('stop_reason') in {'refusal','content_filter'}` for Anthropic) so the two detection conditions are non-overlapping.
findings.finding: The refusal-as-content detection predicate for Anthropic is mechanistically ambiguous: the plan says finish_reason 'normalizes a refusal to content_filter' for Anthropic AND that provider_details 'may carry the raw stop_reason' — but if normalization always produces finish_reason==content_filter, check_stop_reason already catches it (content_filter is in _UNRETRYABLE_STOP_REASONS). The new detection is only needed when finish_reason is NORMAL (e.g. stop) and the refusal signal is carried exclusively in provider_details. The plan does not state this boolean distinction explicitly, so an implementer cannot derive a correct non-overlapping predicate: they may write a check that fires on the already-handled content_filter case (a no-op duplicate) or miss the provider_details-only case (the actual #5221 gap) entirely.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol (concrete, distinct from check_stop_reason)' paragraph; runner.py:497-502 (NativeOutput) and runner.py:519 (PromptedOutput)
findings.suggested_fix: In the detection rule, write: 'The refusal-as-content check is a SECOND guard applied only when `finish_reason` is not in `_UNRETRYABLE_STOP_REASONS` (i.e. `check_stop_reason` passed). It then inspects `provider_details`: for Anthropic, `provider_details.get("stop_reason") in {"refusal", "content_filter"}`; for OpenAI, `provider_details.get("incomplete_details", {}).get("reason") == "content_filter"`. Both require `finish_reason` to be otherwise-normal — that is the precise additive gap over the existing check.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `provider_details` key path to read for Anthropic refusal-as-content detection (e.g., `provider_details.get('stop_reason')` or the nested path from the offline experiment), or document that the Anthropic branch relies solely on `finish_reason == 'content_filter'` with `provider_details` as a corroborating-only check.
findings.finding: The Anthropic-specific `provider_details` key path for refusal-as-content detection is never specified. The plan says `provider_details` 'may carry the raw stop_reason' for Anthropic but names no concrete key to read — in contrast to the OpenAI path, which is fully specified as `provider_details.get('incomplete_details',{}).get('reason') == 'content_filter'`. An implementer cannot write the Anthropic detection branch without knowing which key(s) to inspect.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol (concrete, distinct from check_stop_reason)': "for Anthropic (rebar's provider), `getattr(result.response,'finish_reason',None)` normalizes a refusal to `content_filter` and `provider_details` (a dict, present on `ModelResponse`) may carry the raw `stop_reason`"

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact `provider_details` dict key paths for both Anthropic and OpenAI (e.g. by inspecting a real `ModelResponse` with a known refusal, or reading pydantic-ai source for provider-specific response normalization), and add a concrete code-level assertion or test fixture that exercises the actual key path rather than relying on the inline experiment log.
findings.finding: The plan's refusal-as-content detection for the OpenAI path keys on `provider_details.get('incomplete_details',{}).get('reason') == 'content_filter'`, but `provider_details` on pydantic-ai's `ModelResponse` is listed only in the inline experiment log (no doc link, no code cite, no independent artifact). The specific nested key path `incomplete_details.reason` is Unverified — the experiment log shows the field exists but does not show the dict structure for either the Anthropic or OpenAI provider. If the key path is wrong (e.g. the actual key is `'finish_details'` or structurally different), the refusal-as-content gate silently never fires, which is a correctness failure precisely for the case the plan is trying to close.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: In the implementation, add a debug-log that dumps `provider_details` on any non-`stop` finish reason during the detection check. Write a test fixture that constructs a `ModelResponse` with a canned `provider_details` dict mirroring the actual pydantic-ai OpenAI provider output, asserting the detector fires — then verify the dict structure matches what the live library produces.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the refusal-as-content check is added to BOTH the PromptedOutput and NativeOutput branches (since Anthropic/PromptedOutput is the live path), or explicitly justify why PromptedOutput is already protected against the #5221 failure mode.
findings.finding: The refusal-as-content check is described as being added only to the NativeOutput branch, but rebar's current provider (Anthropic) uses the PromptedOutput branch — meaning the primary live code path for the current provider would remain unprotected against the refusal-as-content failure mode (#5221) that motivates this story.
findings.location: What section: 'Runtime result-validation on BOTH structured output paths' and 'this story adds the same check_stop_reason(...) + refusal-as-content check on the NativeOutput branch'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the What section: 'The refusal-as-content check is added to BOTH branches: NativeOutput (new) and PromptedOutput (augmenting the existing check_stop_reason call there, which currently handles only SDK-signaled refusals with empty parts, not non-empty content with out-of-band filter signals).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add concrete test references for each AC: the file path + test name (or `pytest -k` expression) for (a) the refusal-as-content detection, (b) the truncation-conjunction detection, (c) the false-positive non-fire case, and (d) the schema-guided repair call — or note that these test names are established by the executor (add an AC that a new test file/function with a named pattern must exist).
findings.finding: The AC that says 'RED silent-success cases flip GREEN' (AC5) and 'a test asserts the detector does NOT false-fire on a normal verdict' (AC2 proxy) do not name any test file paths, test function names, or `pytest -k` expressions, so a verifier cannot confirm that the RED baseline is fully covered or that the false-positive test was actually written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 ('RED silent-success cases flip GREEN; make test/make lint/make typecheck green') and 'A test asserts the detector does NOT false-fire...' in the Refusal-as-content protocol paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) to verify the json-repair pin is raised to >=0.61 in pyproject.toml, AND specify a rollback path: if the pin bump causes a dependency conflict, the try/except fallback allows reverting to schema-less repair_json while keeping >=0.30.
findings.finding: The body explicitly states 'this story BUMPS the pin to `json-repair>=0.61`' as a prerequisite for the schema-guided repair argument, but no acceptance criterion verifies the version pin change itself. AC3 checks that `schema=<model>` is called, but if the pin is not bumped the call would fail at runtime in environments with json-repair 0.30–0.60 — the pin bump is load-bearing work with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Body: 'BUMPS the pin to json-repair>=0.61'; Acceptance Criteria section; pyproject.toml
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'pyproject.toml pins json-repair>=0.61; if the pin cannot be satisfied in a consumer environment, the try/except fallback ensures >=0.30 continues to work schema-lessly (expand-contract: the code fallback is the rollback path for the pin change).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete test that imports `json_repair.repair_json` and calls it with `schema=` and `schema_repair_mode=` to assert the parameters exist and the guided repair produces a different (schema-corrected) result from the unguided call, so the >=0.61 floor is proven not just declared.
findings.finding: The plan's `json-repair` schema-guided repair claim — '`repair_json` params include `schema` + `schema_repair_mode` (VERIFIED offline, json-repair 0.61.2)' — cannot be grounded from any repo artifact. The `pyproject.toml` pins `json-repair>=0.30` (confirmed), and the plan proposes bumping to `>=0.61`. But the 'verification' evidence exists only as prose in the plan's own 'Verified library behavior' block; there is no lockfile, no import-introspect fixture, no test exercising `schema=` or `schema_repair_mode`, and no pinned version in the repo that confirms 0.61.2 is installed.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in structured.py' item (1); 'Verified library behavior' block
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_structured.py` or a new `test_json_repair_schema.py`, add a parametrized test that calls `repair_json(malformed_json, return_objects=True, schema=SomeModel.model_json_schema(), schema_repair_mode='strict')` and asserts the result matches the schema — this directly validates both the param existence and the floor bump.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `repair_json` accepts `schema=<model>` and `schema_repair_mode='strict'` parameters, claiming 'VERIFIED offline (json-repair 0.61.2)', but no observable artifact supports this claim: there is no captured --help output, no doc quote, no code citation from json-repair's source, and no test in the repo that exercises these parameters. The only repo usage is `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True)` (structured.py:161), and the current pin is `json-repair>=0.30` — a version where these params may be absent. AC#3 depends directly on this capability.
findings.location: What section — Output self-heal, item (1): 'add `schema=<model-json-schema>, schema_repair_mode=\'strict\'`'; AC#3: '`json_repair` is called with `schema=<model>`'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line verification test (or embed it in the existing `test_structured.py` parametrize table): `from json_repair import repair_json; import inspect; assert 'schema' in inspect.signature(repair_json).parameters`, gated on the bumped pin. This converts the unverified claim to an observed, CI-enforced fact.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The pinned json-repair exposes schema= + schema_repair_mode (verified: repair_json params include schema), so the consumer interface is present; AC confirms the installed version accepts it' — but there is no in-repo experiment log, import probe, unit test, or changelog reference grounding that the installed json-repair>=0.30 version actually supports the schema= and schema_repair_mode parameters. The string 'schema_repair_mode' appears nowhere in the repo. If this parameter was introduced in a later release than >=0.30 covers, or was introduced under a different name, AC item 3 ('json_repair is called with schema=<model>') will silently fail at runtime.
findings.location: What section — Output self-heal bullet (1): 'The pinned json-repair exposes schema= + schema_repair_mode (verified: repair_json params include schema)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC1 into two separate, independently verifiable criteria: one for refusal-as-content detection and one for truncated-but-schema-valid detection, each with its own named test or `pytest -k` expression.
findings.finding: The truncation-within-a-schema-valid-verdict scenario (finish_reason=length AND schema-valid parse) has no distinct proving command beyond 'make test' — AC1 bundles it with the refusal-as-content case, and no step describes a test fixture or assertion that would produce evidence specifically for this code path (the conjunction of `check_stop_reason` detecting 'length' AND `parse_structured` succeeding).
findings.location: AC1 ('Refusal-as-content (via structured signal) and empty/truncated-looks-complete are detected and routed, not returned as a PASS/BLOCK verdict') and 'Truncation-within-a-schema-valid-verdict' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion or explicit test assertion covering the try/except fallback: 'if schema-guided repair_json raises, the schema-less repair_json is invoked and the call does not propagate the exception.'
findings.finding: The body describes a try/except fallback for schema-guided json_repair ('wrapped in a try/except that falls back to the current schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises') — a behavioral safety net that is a genuine deliverable — but no acceptance criterion covers or verifies this fallback path.
findings.location: Body: 'Output self-heal in structured.py' section, structured.py:161 delta description; Acceptance Criteria section
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 to: '`json_repair` is called with `schema=<model>`; if the schema-guided call raises, the schema-less fallback is invoked without error propagation; the targeted reask includes the bounded faulty prior output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit AC (and corresponding test) for each of the three failure/boundary paths: (a) schema-guided `repair_json` exception → fallback executes and produces a valid result; (b) `provider_details` absent or malformed → refusal-as-content detector does not raise and correctly skips; (c) `result.output` is None or empty → faulty-output snippet is omitted or safely replaced in the reask prompt.
findings.finding: The acceptance criteria do not cover failure paths for three newly-introduced behaviors: (a) the try/except fallback when schema-guided `repair_json` raises — no AC verifies the schema-less fallback actually executes and produces a non-regression result; (b) the refusal-as-content detector when `provider_details` is absent entirely or has an unexpected shape — no AC exercises this defensive `getattr`/`.get` path; (c) the faulty-output snippet when `result.output` is `None` or empty — no AC covers these boundary inputs to the new `str(result.output)[:_FAULTY_OUTPUT_SNIPPET_CHARS]` expression.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria; ## What — 'schema-guided repair … wrapped in a try/except'; 'provider_details … may carry the raw stop_reason'; 'str(result.output)[:_FAULTY_OUTPUT_SNIPPET_CHARS]'
findings.suggested_fix: Add three bullet ACs: 'When schema-guided repair_json raises, the fallback to schema-less repair is exercised and the overall call succeeds (regression test vs. current behavior)'; 'When provider_details is absent or incomplete_details key is missing, refusal-as-content detection returns False without raising'; 'When result.output is None or "", the reask prompt omits the prior-output snippet or substitutes a safe placeholder'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exception type(s) to catch in the repair_json schema-guided fallback try/except, and clarify whether the sentinel guard (repaired not in ('', None, [], {})) applies to both the schema-guided result and the fallback result independently.
findings.finding: The plan's edit description for structured.py:161 says 'add schema=<model-json-schema>, schema_repair_mode="strict" for schema-guided deterministic repair before any LLM reask, wrapped in a try/except that falls back to the current schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises'. The actual code at lines 160-163 is a for-loop that calls repair_json once and checks the result against sentinel values. Adding a try/except fallback requires restructuring the loop body into a nested try/except, not simply adding keyword arguments to line 161. The plan does not specify: (a) the exception type(s) to catch in the fallback, or (b) whether the result-sentinel guard (repaired not in ('', None, [], {})) applies to both the schema-guided and fallback calls. Without this, the edit scope at structured.py:160-163 is under-specified.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in src/rebar/llm/structured.py ... (1) structured.py:161 currently calls repair_json ...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (and AC) that `schema=` must receive `model_cls.model_json_schema()` (the JSON Schema dict), not the Pydantic model class itself, and add a test that passes a known-malformed JSON string + schema dict and asserts the repaired output matches the schema.
findings.finding: The plan says `schema=<model-json-schema>` in the experiment-log section but also writes `schema=<model>` in the AC and body. The `json_repair.repair_json` `schema=` parameter (added in 0.61.x) almost certainly expects a JSON Schema dict (as `model_cls.model_json_schema()` returns), NOT a Pydantic model class. The plan never explicitly states which form to pass, leaving this ambiguous. Passing a Pydantic class where a dict is expected would raise a `TypeError` at runtime — and the fallback `try/except` would swallow it, silently regressing to schema-less repair on every call, meaning the feature never activates.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in structured.py', point (1); AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to read: `json_repair is called with schema=model_cls.model_json_schema()`. In the implementation, write `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True, schema=model_cls.model_json_schema(), schema_repair_mode='strict')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define or cite the accepted values for `schema_repair_mode` (e.g., quote the json-repair 0.61.2 docs or source that lists valid modes) and confirm 'strict' is the appropriate choice for the intended 'add missing required fields' / 'remove extra fields' repair behavior.
findings.finding: The `schema_repair_mode` parameter value 'strict' is referenced but its vocabulary (the set of accepted values for this parameter) is never defined by the plan. `repair_json`'s `schema_repair_mode` is a string enum parameter; the plan asserts only that it exists (verified via experiment log parameter list). No definition of what modes are available (e.g., 'strict', 'lenient', 'best_effort') appears in the plan, leaving the implementer unable to validate that 'strict' is a valid, meaningful value for the intended use case.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in src/rebar/llm/structured.py': "add `schema=<model-json-schema>, schema_repair_mode='strict'`"

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exception scope for the schema-guided repair fallback (e.g., catch `Exception` broadly for defensive operation, or enumerate specific json-repair exception classes), and explain the reasoning in the plan.
findings.finding: The try/except fallback for schema-guided `repair_json` is described as 'falls back to the current schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises' but the exception scope is never specified. The plan does not say whether to catch `Exception`, a specific json-repair exception, `TypeError`, or `ValueError`. An overly broad catch (e.g., bare `except Exception`) silently swallows genuine bugs; an overly narrow catch misses json-repair edge cases.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in src/rebar/llm/structured.py': "wrapped in a try/except that falls back to the current schema-less `repair_json` if the schema-guided call raises (so a json-repair edge case never regresses today's behavior)"

findings.finding: The plan states the raised UnretryableOutputError 'carries the mamba LLMOutcome (via classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(finish_reason=...))) as an attribute for messaging/telemetry — one construction, identical to the exception path.' But the plan never identifies WHERE this attachment is made. The existing check_stop_reason (structured.py) does not import or call classify_llm_failure; _pai_structured (runner.py) re-raises UnretryableOutputError immediately without enriching it (line 526: bare `raise`); and the only current LLMOutcome attachment is at runner.py:389 in the generic LLMUnavailableError handler. No new edit location is named for the classify_llm_failure call that attaches the outcome attribute.
findings.location: What section — 'Routing mechanism' paragraph; runner.py:522-526 (bare re-raise of UnretryableOutputError); structured.py:232-243 (check_stop_reason, no failure import)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a catch-enrich-reraise block inside `_pai_structured` in runner.py immediately after the NativeOutput check and in the PromptedOutput loop's `except UnretryableOutputError` branch (replacing the bare `raise`): catch the error, call `classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(finish_reason=finish_reason))`, set `exc.outcome = outcome`, then re-raise. This keeps `structured.py` import-clean and puts the enrichment in the one place (`runner.py`) that already has the failure module imported.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the actual routing intermediaries for UnretryableOutputError (interpreter.py:488 for workflow steps, verify.py:668 for direct verify callers) rather than attributing the INDETERMINATE degrade to gate_dispatch, which has no catch for StructuredOutputError.
findings.finding: The plan attributes the UnretryableOutputError degrade-to-INDETERMINATE routing to gate_dispatch ('routed ... via gate_dispatch'), but gate_dispatch.py only imports and catches LLMUnavailableError — it has no catch block for StructuredOutputError or its subclass UnretryableOutputError. The actual routing intermediaries are interpreter.py:488 (broad 'except Exception' for workflow-step callers) and verify.py:668 (explicit 'except StructuredOutputError' for direct verify callers). These are unenumerated consumers whose behavior determines whether the plan's degrade claim holds.
findings.location: What section — 'Routing mechanism (reuse the existing raise-based degrade)'; runner.py:368-369; gate_dispatch.py:32
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'via gate_dispatch' with 'via the workflow interpreter's per-step broad-except at interpreter.py:488 (for workflow-step callers) and verify.py:668 (for direct verify callers); gate_dispatch has no separate catch and is not the routing agent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the direct `LLMOutcome(...)` construction path retains the totality guarantee of `classify_llm_failure()`'s `except Exception → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION` wrapper (failure.py:273–280); if not, either extend `classify_llm_failure` to accept a `provider_details` signal, or wrap the direct construction in an equivalent guard.
findings.finding: The plan proposes directly constructing `LLMOutcome(resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT, diagnostic=sanitize_diagnostic({...}), retryable=False)` for refusal-as-content cases, explicitly bypassing `classify_llm_failure()`. This partially rebuilds the classification path while losing `classify_llm_failure`'s totality guarantee — the `except Exception → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION` wrapper (failure.py:273–280) that ensures the classifier never raises. The stated justification (that the classifier only maps `content_filter` finish_reason, not provider_details signals) is accurate, but the correct fix is to extend the classifier to accept a `provider_details` signal rather than to fork a separate construction path that drops the safety net.
findings.location: What section — 'Routing mechanism' paragraph; failure.py:259–281
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `provider_refusal: bool = False` parameter to `ClassifyContext` (or a new `refusal_as_content: bool` flag), and handle it in `_map()` as `if ctx.provider_refusal: return ResolutionClass.CHANGE_INPUT` — this routes through the existing totality-guarded path and keeps one classification entry point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add empirical justification (a specific issue number, test case, or experiment log entry showing a field-name/type parse failure that schema-guided repair fixes) before bumping `json-repair>=0.61` and adding `schema=`/`schema_repair_mode='strict'` — or scope this tweak to a follow-on story once such a failure mode is observed.
findings.finding: The plan labels `schema_repair_mode='strict'` with `schema=<model-json-schema>` as a 'proven addition' for the json-repair call, but the only evidence cited is that the parameter exists in json-repair 0.61.2 — not that schema-guided repair improves outcomes for rebar's actual malformed-JSON failure modes. The structured-output research doc (docs/experiments/workflow-remediation-pocs/structured-output-research.md, line 413) recommends `repair_json()` without `schema=`. Rebar's observed parse failures are structural (markdown fences, unclosed braces, array-wrapped single objects) — patterns json-repair already handles well. Schema-guided repair targets field-name/type errors, a different failure class not evidenced in rebar's history.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in structured.py' point (1); Verified library behavior section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (or in an AC) whether the faulty-output snippet is intentionally PromptedOutput-only (acceptable, since Anthropic/rebar uses that path), or whether a mechanism is needed to pass the prior output to pydantic-ai's NativeOutput retry loop.
findings.finding: The plan's line-number reference 'structured.py:161 currently calls `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True)`' is accurate (confirmed), but the companion reference 'runner.py:530's reask currently sends...' is imprecise: the reask prompt string starts at line 529 (the `prompt = (` assignment), not line 530. More materially, the plan describes adding the faulty-output snippet to a reask path that, in the current code, is the ONLY reask path — but the NativeOutput branch (runner.py:497-502) uses pydantic-ai's built-in `retries={'output': OUTPUT_RETRIES}` and never passes through the custom prompt loop at line 529-533, so the faulty-output snippet addition described in the plan only applies to the PromptedOutput path. The plan does not acknowledge this NativeOutput/PromptedOutput asymmetry for the faulty-output snippet.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in structured.py' item (2); runner.py:497-502 and runner.py:529-533

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `schema=` expects `model_cls.model_json_schema()` (a dict), not the Pydantic class itself, and make this explicit in the plan's code-level description of the structured.py:161 change.
findings.finding: The `schema=<model>` argument to `repair_json` uses a placeholder `<model>` that is ambiguous: `json_repair.repair_json`'s `schema=` parameter expects a JSON Schema dict (the output of `model_cls.model_json_schema()`), NOT a Pydantic model class instance. The plan's notation `schema=<model>` does not clarify this, and an implementer who passes the Pydantic class directly would get a type error or silent misbehavior.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in src/rebar/llm/structured.py': "add `schema=<model-json-schema>, schema_repair_mode='strict'`" and "repair_json(cand, return_objects=True) WITHOUT a `schema=` arg — add `schema=<model-json-schema>`"
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan's notation to: `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True, schema=model_cls.model_json_schema(), schema_repair_mode='strict')`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structured log line (e.g., `logger.warning('silent_success_detected', check=..., finish_reason=..., provider_details_key=...)`) and/or a counter metric at each new detection site (refusal-as-content and truncation-conjunction) so operators can observe detection rates and distinguish them from pre-existing INDETERMINATE routes.
findings.finding: The plan adds two new silent-success detection paths (refusal-as-content via provider_details, truncation-within-a-valid-verdict) that can fire in production, but specifies no observability — no structured log line, metric counter, or trace attribute — to record when these checks activate. Operators cannot distinguish a legitimate INDETERMINATE routed by the new checks from one routed by pre-existing paths, and silent failures in the detection logic itself (e.g., provider_details format drift) produce no signal.
findings.location: Plan section 'What' — Refusal-as-content protocol and Routing mechanism paragraphs; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: At the point where UnretryableOutputError is raised for the new checks, emit a structured log entry with at minimum: check name ('refusal_as_content' | 'truncation_in_valid_verdict'), the raw finish_reason, whether provider_details was present, and the model_name. If rebar already has a metrics facade, increment a counter tagged with the check name. This is a one-liner at each raise site.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `json-repair` exposes a `schema=` parameter and `schema_repair_mode` ('verified: `repair_json` params include `schema`'), but no experiment log, doc quote, or code citation in the repo backs this claim. The existing research doc describes json-repair solely as a syntactic fixer (fences/commas/quotes); `schema_repair_mode` and `schema=` are not mentioned anywhere. The pinned version (`>=0.30`) is consistent with the basic fixer, not a schema-aware variant. If these parameters don't exist or behave differently, AC 3 ('`json_repair` is called with `schema=<model>`') cannot be satisfied.
findings.location: What section — output self-heal delta (1); AC item 3; `src/rebar/llm/structured.py:161`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line offline experiment to the story's experiment log: `from json_repair import repair_json; import inspect; print(inspect.signature(repair_json))`. If `schema=` is absent, replace this delta with post-repair Pydantic coercion (which the existing `validate_to` already provides) and update AC 3 accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the decision to bypass `classify_llm_failure` for refusal-as-content (and the per-provider `provider_details` key paths) in an ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or inline design-doc comment block so future maintainers understand why direct `LLMOutcome` construction is correct here.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decision to bypass `classify_llm_failure` and directly construct `LLMOutcome` — and the provider-specific `provider_details` detection rules for Anthropic vs. OpenAI — are described only in the plan narrative but are not captured in an ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update. Future maintainers encountering this pattern could misread it as a bug or 'simplify' it by routing through the classifier, silently breaking the refusal-as-content detection.
findings.location: Plan section 'What' — Routing mechanism paragraph; file runner.py refusal-as-content detection
findings.suggested_fix: Add a CLAUDE.md section (or `## Architecture Decision` inline doc block in runner.py above the detection code) explaining: (1) why classify_llm_failure is NOT used (it maps finish_reason only, but refusal-as-content has normal finish_reason with the signal in provider_details), (2) the per-provider key paths and their stability assumptions, (3) the truncation-conjunction rule. A one-paragraph CLAUDE.md entry suffices.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether the schema-guided `repair_json` + faulty-output-snippet self-heal tweaks (orthogonal to the detection/routing path) warrant a separate child ticket, given they fire on a completely different execution branch and could be verified independently.
findings.finding: The plan bundles two independently-valuable, independently-releasable concerns: (1) silent-success DETECTION & routing (NativeOutput path fix + truncation-conjunction + refusal-as-content, all raising `UnretryableOutputError`), and (2) output self-HEAL quality tweaks (schema-guided `repair_json` fallback + faulty-output snippet in reask). These fire on orthogonal execution paths — detection degrades with no reask, self-heal tweaks improve the existing reask path — and could each ship and be verified independently.
findings.location: ## What — first and fourth bullet; ## Why — 'two proven additions'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(finish_reason=...)) -> resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT' for detected silent-success cases (truncation and refusal). The actual failure.py _map function (lines 229-256) only maps ctx.finish_reason=='content_filter' to CHANGE_INPUT; finish_reason values of 'length', 'max_tokens', and 'refusal' all fall through to the final catch-all returning NEEDS_INVESTIGATION. The plan's routing claim is false for the truncation and refusal finish_reason values, which are the primary cases this story handles.
findings.location: What section, routing mechanism paragraph: 'classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(finish_reason=...)) -> resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT + sanitized diagnostic'
findings.suggested_fix: In failure.py _map, extend the finish_reason guard from `if ctx.finish_reason in ('content_filter',)` to `if ctx.finish_reason in ('content_filter', 'length', 'max_tokens', 'refusal'):` so all unretryable stop reasons map to CHANGE_INPUT, matching the plan's stated intent.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that detected silent-success cases are routed via `classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(finish_reason=...)) -> resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT`, but the actual `_map()` function in `failure.py` has no branch for `UnretryableOutputError`. An `UnretryableOutputError` raised by the new detection logic will fall through to the `NEEDS_INVESTIGATION` default — not `CHANGE_INPUT` as claimed. The decision rule in the plan conflicts with the classifier schema it references.
findings.location: What section — 'Routing mechanism' paragraph: 'classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(finish_reason=...)) -> resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT + sanitized diagnostic'
findings.suggested_fix: Pass `exc=None, ctx=ClassifyContext(finish_reason='content_filter')` to `classify_llm_failure` for refusal-as-content cases, and `exc=None, ctx=ClassifyContext(finish_reason='length')` for truncation cases — the existing `_map()` guard at line 232 already returns `CHANGE_INPUT` for `content_filter` when exc is None. Alternatively, add `isinstance(exc, UnretryableOutputError)` to _map() and return CHANGE_INPUT.

findings.finding: AC#2 states this story delivers 'A verdict schema-shape check replaces any non-empty output heuristic,' but the plan body explicitly says parse_structured's schema validation 'already a shape check against the verdict model, not a non-empty heuristic' — implying no replacement is needed and the AC describes pre-existing behavior as a deliverable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC#2 vs. 'What' section paragraph 1
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC#2 with: 'Schema-valid output that also carries a length/max_tokens finish_reason is treated as degraded (not PASS/BLOCK); a schema-valid, untruncated verdict is the explicit pass condition. A test asserts that a clean zero-finding verdict with no truncation signal passes through correctly.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
